title: How to Calculate Your APS Score for University Admission in South Africa
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your National Senior Certificate results give you a percentage for each subject and a level from 1 to 7. But South African universities do not make admission decisions based on your percentages alone. They convert your results into an Admission Point Score, or APS. The APS is a number that represents your overall academic achievement and determines whether you meet the minimum requirements for the courses you have applied for. Understanding how your APS is calculated helps you set realistic targets and make informed decisions about where to apply.

How the APS Is Calculated

The APS is calculated by assigning a point value to each of your NSC subjects based on the achievement level you earned. A Level 7, representing eighty percent and above, is worth seven points. A Level 6, seventy to seventy-nine percent, is worth six points. A Level 5, sixty to sixty-nine percent, is worth five points. A Level 4, fifty to fifty-nine percent, is worth four points. A Level 3, forty to forty-nine percent, is worth three points. A Level 2, thirty to thirty-nine percent, is worth two points. A Level 1, below thirty percent, is worth one point.

Your total APS is the sum of the points from your best six subjects, excluding Life Orientation. Life Orientation is not included in the APS calculation at most South African universities. This means your APS is calculated from six subjects, not seven. The maximum possible APS is therefore forty-two points, representing six subjects at Level 7. This is an important detail. A student who performs well in Life Orientation but poorly in their other subjects may find that their strong Life Orientation mark does not help their APS at all.

Different universities may calculate the APS slightly differently. Some include Life Orientation but weight it at half the value of other subjects. Some calculate the APS from seven subjects rather than six. Check the specific calculation method for each university you are applying to. The information is published on the university's website or in its undergraduate prospectus. Do not assume that all universities calculate the APS the same way.

What APS Score You Need for Different Courses

The minimum APS required varies by university and by course. A Bachelor's degree typically requires an APS of twenty-three to twenty-eight points, depending on the university and the field of study. A diploma typically requires an APS of nineteen to twenty-three points. A higher certificate typically requires an APS of fifteen to nineteen points. These are minimums. Meeting the minimum does not guarantee admission. Competitive courses at top South African universities often require significantly higher APS scores than the published minimum.

Health sciences courses, including Medicine, Nursing, and Pharmacy, are among the most competitive. An APS of thirty-five or higher is often needed for Medicine, along with strong performance in Mathematics, Physical Sciences, and Life Sciences specifically. Engineering courses typically require an APS of thirty or higher, with strong performance in Mathematics and Physical Sciences. Commerce courses at leading universities may require an APS of twenty-eight to thirty-two, with strong Mathematics results. Humanities and social science courses typically have lower APS requirements, often between twenty-three and twenty-eight.

The APS is not the only criterion for admission. Universities also consider your performance in specific subjects relevant to your chosen course. A student with a high overall APS but weak Mathematics may not qualify for Engineering, even though the APS number looks strong. The subject-specific requirements are as important as the total score.

How to Use Your APS When Applying

Calculate your APS early, using your Grade 11 final results and your Grade 12 mid-year results as estimates. The estimated APS tells you which courses you are likely to qualify for and which are likely out of reach. It helps you set realistic goals for your final examinations. If your estimated APS is significantly below the requirement for your intended course, you have time to adjust your preparation or to explore alternative courses.

Apply to a range of institutions with different APS requirements. Include courses where your estimated APS is well above the minimum as safety options. Include courses where your estimated APS is close to the typical competitive score as target options. Include one or two courses where your estimated APS is slightly below the competitive score as reach options, but only if your final examination performance could realistically close the gap. A balanced application strategy increases your chances of receiving at least one offer.

The Bottom Line

Your APS is calculated from your best six NSC subjects, excluding Life Orientation at most South African universities. Each achievement level from 1 to 7 is worth the corresponding number of points. Bachelor's degree programs typically require an APS of twenty-three or higher, with competitive courses requiring thirty or above. Calculate your estimated APS early using your Grade 11 and Grade 12 mid-year results. Apply to a range of courses with different APS requirements. The APS is one factor in university admission, along with subject-specific performance and, at some institutions, National Benchmark Test results.
